findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 31. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: Ensure the main landmark is at top level
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  The main landmark is contained in another landmark. [<main>]

findings.description: Ensure the document has at most one main landmark
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  Document has more than one main landmark [<main>]

Fix any of the following:
  The landmark must have a unique aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title to make landmarks distinguishable [<main>]

findings.description: Table cells in the HTML are excessively tall with line-wrapped content that breaks readability of the table structure. Column headers and data cells that are single-line in the PDF are wrapped into multiple lines in the HTML, making the table visually broken and difficult to read.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a compact, well-aligned table with single-line entries for Owner-Facility, County, Region, etc. HTML shows the same data but with extreme row heights due to word-wrapping in narrow columns (e.g., 'Linville Ridge Country Club - Linville Ridge Country Club WWTP' spans 5+ lines in HTML vs 2 lines in PDF).

findings.description: The HTML renders the table in a significantly narrower layout than the PDF, causing column compression and excessive vertical expansion of rows throughout all pages.
findings.evidence: PDF has a wide landscape-oriented table spanning the full page width with readable columns. HTML compresses this into a narrow portrait-style layout where columns like 'Owner - Facility' and 'Case Number' are squeezed, causing multi-line wrapping throughout.
